title: Catechist Guide — Matrimony
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 60 minutes

Core theme: Matrimony is the sacrament of the irrevocable covenant between a man and a woman, raised by Christ to an efficacious sign of his love for the Church. Founded «from the beginning» in creation, wounded by sin and restored in Christ, Christian marriage rests on four pillars: unity, indissolubility, fidelity, and openness to life.

Objectives:

  • Understand that marriage is not a human invention but part of God's creative plan.
  • Discover how Christ elevates natural marriage to a sacrament.
  • Know the essential properties: unity, indissolubility, fidelity, openness to life.
  • Understand consent as the form of the sacrament and the spouses as ministers.
  • Value the family as the «domestic church» and address contemporary challenges with truth and mercy.

Key verse: «What therefore God has joined together, let not man separate» (Mt 19:6) and «Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her» (Eph 5:25)

Catechism references: CCC 1601-1666

Suggested Lesson Structure

Section Time What the Catechist Does
1. Big Question (Louis and Zélie Martin) 8 min Tell the story of St. Thérèse's parents. Highlight that they were canonized together as a married couple. Ask: «Can human love reflect God's love?»
2. «From the Beginning»: the original plan 7 min Read Gen 1:27-28 and Gen 2:18-25. Explain marriage as a communion of persons created in God's image. Highlight the three verbs: leave, cling, become one flesh.
3. Under sin and God's pedagogy 7 min Explain how sin wounds marriage (polygamy, divorce, domination). Show God's pedagogy through Hosea and the Song of Songs: the spousal metaphor of the covenant.
4. Christ elevates marriage to a sacrament 8 min Read Mt 19:1-12 (dialogue with the Pharisees). Jesus abrogates the Mosaic concession and restores indissolubility. Read Eph 5:25-33: connection with sacramentum.
5. Cana and the four pillars 10 min Tell Jn 2:1-11: Christ sanctifies the wedding. Explain unity, indissolubility, fidelity, openness to life. Cite Humanae Vitae.
6. Consent and the domestic church 7 min The spouses are the ministers. Explain free consent, canonical form. The family as a school of prayer and virtue.
7. Contemporary challenges 7 min Address divorce, cohabitation, same-sex unions with doctrinal truth and pastoral mercy. Distinguish welcoming the person from the truth about marriage.
8. Reflection, Key Takeaway, and closing 6 min Reflection questions individually or in pairs. Read the Key Takeaway aloud. Pray together for marriages.

Notes for the Catechist

The Big Question: Louis and Zélie Martin

The story of St. Thérèse's parents grabs attention immediately. They were ordinary people with a vocation to marriage lived heroically. He wanted to be a Carthusian monk; she was rejected by the Sisters of Charity. God brought them together. They had nine children; only five survived (all girls, all Carmelites). Zélie died young of cancer. Louis suffered dementia and was institutionalized. And yet their love was fruitful beyond imagination: one daughter is a Doctor of the Church. They were the first spouses beatified together as a married couple (2008) and canonized together (2015). The question that opens the class —«Can human love reflect God's love?»— is answered by their lives: not only can it, but God raises it to a sacrament.

«From the Beginning»: Hermeneutical Key

Jesus does not cite Deuteronomy or discuss rabbinical schools. He cites Genesis. This is revolutionary. The indissolubility of marriage is not a late ecclesiastical imposition: it is inscribed in creation itself. Marriage is not a cultural invention that the Church blessed; it is a natural reality —man and woman created for fruitful communion— that Christ restored and elevated. This point is essential for dismantling the idea that the Church «invented» indissolubility.

The Four Pillars: Not «Rules» but Freedoms

It is wise to present unity, indissolubility, fidelity, and openness to life not as a list of prohibitions but as the necessary conditions for love to truly be love. A love that tolerates «other options» (infidelity) is no longer love. A love «as long as it works» (divorce) is not love; it is convenience. A love closed to life impoverishes itself. Presenting it this way transforms perception: from «the Church forbids» to «the Church protects the truth of love.»

Humanae Vitae: The Prophetic Document

Pope St. Paul VI's encyclical (1968) accurately predicted what would follow from the widespread use of contraception: loss of respect for women (objectification), trivialization of sexuality, state impositions of anti-natalist policies. Its positive proposal —natural family planning— has been backed by science. Many catechists have never read it. A brief citation can surprise students who think the Church only «prohibits.»

The Spouses as Ministers: A Unique Singularity

Among all the sacraments, Matrimony is unique in that the ministers are the contracting parties themselves. The priest is a qualified witness. This theological truth often surprises and helps people understand why consent is the heart of marriage: it is an act of will that creates the bond. The consent formula —«I take you to be my husband/wife»— deserves to be read and discussed in class.

Contemporary Challenges: Truth and Mercy

This section requires balance. On divorce: do not deny the real suffering of many, but distinguish pastoral accompaniment from doctrinal change. On cohabitation: many young people live together today; do not condemn, but propose the beauty of a definitive commitment before the total gift of sexuality. On same-sex unions: the Church welcomes persons with same-sex attraction («compassion, sensitivity,» CCC 2358) and rejects every form of unjust discrimination, but cannot call «marriage» what does not correspond to God's plan. This distinction —the person is sacred; the act and its equivalence cannot be approved— must be explained with clarity and charity.

Adaptation by Age Group

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

At this age, marriage is their parents' and grandparents' marriage. Ask them: «What do you like most about how your mom and dad love each other?» Talk about the family as a «little church»: «Do you pray at home? Do you have a cross on the wall?» The story of the wedding at Cana —Jesus at a party, saving a wedding— connects very well. Tell the story of Louis and Zélie Martin and ask: «Do you know that St. Thérèse's parents are saints?» Adapt the circle-drawing activity: have them draw their family and put Jesus in the center. Do not address contemporary challenges directly; if a question comes up, answer with simplicity.

🌿 Shoots (teens 13-17)

Adolescence is when they begin asking questions about love and sexuality. This topic is crucial. Culture tells them sex is for pleasure and love is a feeling. The Church tells them love is total self-giving that requires fidelity and fruitfulness. There may be resistance. Use science: studies show that pre-marital cohabitation increases the likelihood of divorce. Talk about chastity as a path of freedom, not repression: «knowing how to wait is knowing how to love.» Mention the difference between infatuation (a passing feeling) and love (an act of will that chooses the other every day). The film Fireproof can be a powerful resource: a firefighter who learns that love is action, not feeling.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Many adults in catechumenate or recent converts come with complex marital histories: divorced and remarried, cohabitation, children from different partners. It is essential to welcome without judging and to explain doctrine as a path to fullness, not condemnation. Emphasize that the Church is not a museum for saints but a field hospital for sinners. Doctrine is the truth that sets free, even if it hurts at first. Mercy and truth are not contradictory, as Amoris Laetitia teaches. If there are people in irregular situations, invite them to speak privately with the catechist or a priest. They must not be denied community welcome, without prejudice to sacramental discipline.

🌳 Tree (well-formed adults)

Go deeper into sacramental theology: consent as the form and matter of the sacrament (St. Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologiae Suppl., q.42-46). Explain the concept of «contract» vs «covenant» (berit) in Scripture and how Vatican II recovered the language of covenant. Discuss the magisterial documents: Casti Connubii (Pius XI, 1930), Humanae Vitae (Paul VI, 1968), Familiaris Consortio (John Paul II, 1981), the «Theology of the Body» of John Paul II, Amoris Laetitia (Francis, 2016). Relate marriage to other vocations —virginity, priestly celibacy—: both are paths of total, lifelong spousal love. Explain the social dimension of marriage: the family is the basic cell of society; without stable families there is no healthy society.

Additional Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 260-271 («What is marriage?», «Why does the Church oppose divorce?»)
  • Compendium of the Catechism: Questions 337-350
  • Pope St. John Paul II: Theology of the Body — the Wednesday catecheses (1979-1984). Essential for understanding the Catholic vision of sexuality and marriage.
  • Pope Francis: Amoris Laetitia (2016) — apostolic exhortation on the family. Particularly chapters 4 (love in marriage: 1 Cor 13 applied) and 8 (accompanying, discerning, integrating).
  • Pope St. Paul VI: Humanae Vitae (1968) — brief (31 paragraphs) and prophetic.
  • Video: «Marriage Explained» by Catholic Link on YouTube.
  • Film: Fireproof (2008) — ideal for watching and discussing in groups.
  • Book: Edward Sri, Men, Women, and the Mystery of Love — practical application of the Theology of the Body.
  • Testimony: «The Martins: Saints in the Family» — a short documentary about Louis and Zélie.

Difficult Questions

«Why doesn't the Church allow divorce if Jesus spoke about mercy?»

Because it was Jesus himself —the same Jesus who spoke of mercy— who said: «What God has joined together, let no man separate» (Mt 19:6). Indissolubility is not a Church invention; it is an explicit teaching of Christ. Jesus does not pit mercy against indissolubility. Mercy welcomes wounded persons —and the Church must do so—, but it cannot bless what Christ declared contrary to God's plan. That said, the Church recognizes that there are situations of rupture where civil divorce may be necessary to protect rights (alimony, custody). The annulment process examines whether the marriage was actually valid from the start. Amoris Laetitia invites a pastoral accompaniment that distinguishes between doctrine (which does not change) and pastoral discipline (which can be adapted according to circumstances).

«This 'openness to life' sounds like we have to have twelve kids. Isn't that irresponsible?»

The Church does not require having twelve children, nor does she prohibit family planning. She teaches that the generation of life should be done with generosity and responsibility: spouses, in dialogue with God, discern when and how many children to have. But this discernment must respect the language of the body: every conjugal act must remain open to life. This is why the Church proposes natural methods of fertility awareness (sympto-thermal method, Billings method): they allow spacing births without breaking the unity between love and life. Humanae Vitae calls this «responsible parenthood.» It is not a binary choice between «twelve kids» and «contraception.» There is a middle path that respects the integrity of the conjugal act.

«My cousin married a Protestant and they're happy. What's wrong with that?»

The Church recognizes the validity of mixed marriages (Catholic + baptized non-Catholic) with the proper dispensation. The Catholic spouse commits to raising the children in the Catholic faith. Many mixed marriages are beautiful witnesses of love and respect. But one must be realistic about the difficulties: differences about the Eucharist (receiving communion together?), about contraception, about the religious education of children. The Church asks for honesty: not ignoring doctrinal differences but facing them with love and truth. Happiness is not the sole criterion for truth. Mixed marriage requires special preparation and accompaniment.

«Why can't two men or two women who love each other get married in the Church? Isn't that discrimination?»

The Church teaches that persons with same-sex attraction must be treated with «respect, compassion, and sensitivity» and that «every sign of unjust discrimination in their regard should be avoided» (CCC 2358). But marriage, by its nature, requires the man-woman complementarity inscribed in creation (Gen 1:27; Gen 2:24). It is not that the Church arbitrarily decided to exclude anyone; it is that marriage, by definition, is the union of one man and one woman open to life. What is sometimes perceived as a «no» is really an affirmation of a truth about the human person that the Church has no authority to change. Welcoming the person does not require approving everything the person does. The Church distinguishes between the inclination (which may be involuntary), the acts (which are morally assessable), and the claim to equate homosexual unions with marriage (which is doctrinally impossible).

«We're living together before marriage. Is it really that serious?»

The Church understands that many couples cohabit today for economic or cultural reasons. But pastorally she invites a rediscovery of the value of pre-marital chastity. The reason is not prudishness: it is about protecting the freedom and truth of love. When two people live together without a definitive commitment, they are telling each other with their bodies: «I give myself totally to you,» but with the mental reservation: «if it doesn't work out, I'm gone.» That is not total love; it is conditional love. Chastity during engagement is not repression; it is preparation for the total gift that will be sealed in marriage. Many couples who choose this path testify that it deepened their dialogue, their mutual respect, and their interior freedom. The Church proposes, she does not impose.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus, Bridegroom of the Church,
who at Cana sanctified the wedding feast
and on the Cross sealed your covenant with humanity,
give me grace to communicate the beauty of Christian marriage
with truth and tenderness.
May my students discover
that human love, when lived in you,
is a sacrament of your infinite love.
Grant me wisdom for difficult questions,
a heart for those who suffer in their relationships,
and courage to proclaim the truth without mutilating it.
May I see in every wounded marriage
not an argument against the doctrine,
but a heart that needs to be healed by you.
I pray for all the spouses of the world,
especially those in crisis.
Give them the grace you gave to Louis and Zélie,
to Joseph and Mary:
the grace to love to the uttermost,
trusting that you make all things new.
Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
